notes.note: The Fourth Catilinarian was delivered before the Senate in the Temple of Concord beside the Forum on 5 December 63 BC, the day of the debate on the conspirators' punishment; the conspirators were executed in the Tullianum that night.
D. Iunius Silanus, consul-elect for 62 BC, opened the senatorial debate by voting for the supreme penalty (death).

C. Iulius Caesar, then praetor-elect, proposed instead lifelong imprisonment in Italian municipia and confiscation of property. Caesar's intervention is reported at length by Sallust as well as in this speech; it forced Cicero to mount the great defence of the senatorial death-sentence that follows.
Cicero commends his then-two-year-old son to the Senate's care --- a famous closing flourish, contrasting the consul's risk with his hope for the next generation.
